author: @TheSuperChannel
text:
⬛️  2 YEARS AGO, When I saw this, I did an experiment. 

1. I attempted to make Graphene from bamboo in a home made autoclave I saw from another one of your Videos. 
2. I crushed up the bamboo carbon into a powder. 
3. I made this home made mold-able plastic with PVA from my local home depot hardware superstore and borax, ( some thing i had plenty of from using a borax solution to soak the season before,  freshly cut bamboo in to clean and preserve it. 
4. I used the carbonized bamboo graphene / graphite powder as the filler in the above moldable plastic solution and had a very moldable black yet almost metalic silly puddy that I did my best to press into a home made silicone tube silicone mold. 

Let me tell you something, I HAVE NOT BEEN ABLE TO BREAK that plastic since it dried yet. 

My original purpose was to attempt to make natural moldable graphene reinforced JOINTS for a bamboo bicycle instead of using the standard usual steal and or metal. While I have not don't the joints yet. AS HARD AS THIS PLASTIC still is, I will be casting mold joints, i need to use either another additional filler, as the original mix was way way too much silly putty like for me or straight bamboo shavings as its own natural tensile strength seem to be comparable to steal anyways and also in conjunction to make it mostly bamboo anyways.

Does this sound crazy? Any additional filler pointers would be welcome. Thank you for your share, knowledge, and your viewers who all see to be as excited as you make us about science and a great future. Thank you. :-)

author: @chaitanyabhave2092
text: I tried this a couple days back using an iron oxide(I think it's goethite) as the filler, no glycerin. I think I must have added less borax than needed because I've ended up with a rather tough, rubber-like material. It's quite elastic, but doesn't quite snap back, instead just sort of oozes back into shape. But it can take a beating from a screwdriver and hammer without significant damage.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate

replies.author: @chaitanyabhave2092
replies.text: I also made one with iron oxide nanoparticles (Tea stabilized); it too is very tough indeed. I'm thinking of trying nano-clay particles and alumina as well. I'm having a slight problem though. My plastic doesn't quite form as a single lump, instead forming as small flakes, similar to how cottage cheese looks. Could this be because I'm using boric acid and not borax?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes mate - or more likely you got the ratios just a little off

replies.author: @chaitanyabhave2092
replies.text: Oh. Yes that's very likely.

author: @deangermeten5629
text: From reading some old recipes in Henley's manual, I believe adding Al Sulfate will make it water-proof.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's very cool info mate - do you have a link to the manual or the full title?

replies.author: @deangermeten5629
replies.text: Henley's Formulas for Home & Workshop (C1927) bottom p. 378; the original formula was discovered accidentally, mixing ordinary animal glue with alum. Animal glue is a mix of protein with silica. May need to add silica to your plastic to get a similar reaction.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
cheers mate i found this free download https://archive.org/details/henleystwentieth00hiscrich
is this the one?

replies.author: @deangermeten5629
replies.text: yep.

replies.author: @deangermeten5629
replies.text: So I mixed up some aluminum sulfate with just enough water to dissolve into a thick, milky solution and added PVA (no borax) and stirred it up, it formed a ball of of non-tacky plastic straight-away.  I poured the solution off and have a marble drying as we speak. I could see working graphene into the PVA before mixing and then pressing flat into super-capacitor plates. The Al2-(SO4)3 should act as as an ionic salt/polarizable solid electrolyte.

I finally did the experiment. I used
 tb  powder  PolivinI Alcohol, 
1 tb Cornstarch mixed into a cup that contained 2 tb Boxa into a  150 ml Hot Water.

The reaction was not as expected. I was hoping to see a jelly type of mass. 

Maybe, I had the wrong PVA. in a separate spoon, I poured a little PolivinI Alcohol in powder. And I added a little water. I was hoping to see the PVA disolve in water (in the spoon) and become slime. However, this became like a thick rubber. 

my intention is to make a rubber like  fabric by combining Borax, PVA and powder such as Cornstarch

replies.author: @peterciurea7771
replies.text: Citric acid is great for crosslinking PVA - it stays unlinked until you use some alkali like NaOH to harden it and it resists being unlinked after that. Mix your PVA and citric- paint it on a membrane like felt or some similar, then dunk it in a sodium hydroxide solution for  while. Press it between 2 sheets of hard film to normalize thickness and then wash it with water or some weak hcl to eliminate any unreacted alkali. Voila: Proton Exchange Membrane- environmentally responsible.

author: @dominotr
text: I'm coming to the party a bit late, but question: Must there be a filler?  If I just wanted a more translucent polymer would that work with just the pva/water/glycerin mix and borax solution?  And second question, would it be something that could be oven baked at 300F?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Domino Truitt there doesn't have to be a filler but adding a filler creates a composite that can have quite startling effects. If you leave the filler out expect quite a bit of shrinkage as the mix is water heavy - I have never tried baking it but it is an idea worth trying - though you might want to expect some nasty smells!!

replies.author: @dominotr
replies.text: Thanks so much Robert.  I've been experimenting with some alternative plastics.  I want strength, sheerness/translucency and durability.  I'm leaning toward uv cured resins, but trying to fiind a bio version of that seems to be tough.  Bio plastic recipes everywhere, but uv bio resins not so much.  I make jewelry, and want a medium for the creation of hollow polymer beads.  So you can see my challenge.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Domino Truitt yes I can - you might want to try gelatin with ammonium ferric citrate - the green one not the brown - it is a food additive

replies.author: @dominotr
replies.text: wow, ok, and that will give me a bio resin that will uv cure?  would you happen to have the ratios and recipe setup?  ie., gelatin blooming required or no?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Domino Truitt it is a uv cure i experimented with it a while ago so i will have to dig out my lab notes for exact details but it is easy to experiment with

author: @chrisnorth3458
text: Hi Robert, please don't offer me a glass of water! I fear a trip to your house could result in poisoning! haha. Do you think this material once dry would suit any kind of machining tasks? milling, drilling holes or would it suit molding only? Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the machinability would depend on the additives you put in - so if add a lot of talc it will sand and machine really well - if you add sand it will be pretty poor

author: @res1492
text: thanks, could you clear coat it to make it water resistant, i love helping my son with his mid term homeworks and most of them involve making models, this would be great for that, also is it non toxic..?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: clear cote would definitely work. I am not sure about the toxicity. But the LD50 is in the order of kilos. It won't taste that wonderful but borax is used for laundry and PVA is just white glue. So, if toxicity worries you check out the MSDS for both of those Google will help here.

I went to Home Depot and the only thing I found was the PVA wall primer, and I tried that but it didn't work. It claims to use Polyvinyl Acetate but I've had no results, even after I tried converting it to PV Alcohol. Is the concentration too little or something? Thank you so much, and thanks for sharing all these wonderful videos!

Do you use a mold release and if so what are you using. Thank you and looking forward to seeing more of your videos.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hiya, I am British, hence the accent - glad you like it - however my wife is American and you can get PVA quite easily - it is Elmer's white wood glue. The white school glue is the same stuff. Get Elmer's and see what it is like then sourcing it will be very easy for you. Elmer's is a little expensive but once you know what i am on about you will recognise it in an instant. The go to home depot and look in the paint stabilizers you can buy 1 gallon packs very easily and cheaply.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: i'm not sure mate - i'll have a look

author: @unanimoose
text: awesome... are there a way to make it clear?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Lol, bless your heart Mary. The problem is that there is a million different formulations for PVA. All will work but will need a little tweaking to get them to work. So, basically, pop down to the store and get a tub of PVA. You can buy it in gallon tubs for about 10 dollars from target, home depot or walmart pour out 10 glasses and try different strengths of borax solution and see what happens. you'll get there and experimentation is fun!

replies.author: @clarencementor4862
replies.text: I think he said that he used PVA glue which is 50/50 mixture of polyvinyl alcohol of polyvinyl acetate. The borax forms cross-links between these two substances to make the plastic with the PVA glue, water, talc filler and glycerol mix.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hiya mate, Chances are there is just too much water in the PVA you are using. Try drying it out or switching to a more concentrated type. The stuff i used is builders PVA and is meant to be watered down beor use so it is pretty concentrated. What country are you from? I might be able to point you in the right direction. Rob

author: @PaddyS011
text:
Hi Robert - thanks so much for your fantastic collection of videos.  Im learning a lot from following your instructions. Im having a bit of trouble with this one though. I keep ending up with a slime/putty rather than a plastic. Im using all the same ingredients, but maybe you could suggest something to try?

I know its not specific measurements, but maybe you could give a few rough ratios/measurements to keep me right?

Thanks again

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Yeah no problem. But, remember this is not so much a recipe as a base for experimentation. to make it you need PVA, water, borax and some kind of filler. The filler can be just about anything. I used sand, clay and talcum powder. You can use lots of things and a different filler will change the properties of the end product. So, play around with it and put up some vids - i'd love to see

replies.author: @Serenelove520

Your kind offer of help is well appreciated too - like all of us working on this stuff my main lack is money! - it would help if your members clicked on an ad or two as i am a youtube partner - i don't get a fortune from it - ($100 in a year - but if i got more I would spend it on the equipment I need)

author: @TheCuriousSeb
text: Because cement is chemically reactive with water it seemed to prevent the PVA/borax reaction to occur. So as you said, the filler needs to be chemically inert. I tried it with a sand mix but I believe I may have mixed too much borax in as the product was a bit crumbly.

author: @TheCuriousSeb
text: Cheers. My supplies should be here mid-next week so I can try this. As well as trying inert fillers I am curious as to how a cement filler would work. I will keep you posted if it works out any good.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: no - what i have is graphite oxide - i need to disperse it in water first to make graphene oxide - the difference between the two is layer thickness - graphite oxide is relatively thick - graphene is one or two layers thick.
